coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of GQDs/W18O49/tetragonal WO3 homostructures for improving the photoelectric properties
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Peng
Lei, Yun
Wu, Yuncui
Li, Can
Du, Beibei
Wang, Yongqin
Luo, Linhui
Zou, Bingsuo
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as excellent conductive materials could effectively enhance the electron transmission efficiency of W18O49 nanofibers after their combination. In order to investigate the effect of different crystal phase WO3 on the optoelectric properties of GQDs/W18O49 composites, the orthogonal phase WO3 (o-WO3), hexagonal phase WO3 (h-WO3) and tetragonal phase WO3 (t-WO3) had been used to produce GQDs/W18O49/WO3 homojunction with GQDs/W18O49. After comparing them, it was found that the photocurrent intensity of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was 2.35 times, 1.68 times and 1.08 times as high as that of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 at 1.2 V versus Hg/Hg2Cl2, respectively. And the t-WO3 could effectively decrease the band gap, reduce the interfacial resistance, prolong the electron lifetime, accelerate the electron conduction and increase the photocurrent magnitude of the homojunction. The photoelectric performance of the composites could be adjusted by regulating the ratios of GQDs/W18O49 and t-WO3, the ratio 2:1 of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites presented a higher photocurrent density and lower interfacial resistance than other proportions. The physical mechanism of such enhanced photoconduction in this homostructure have been discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
W18O49
WO3
Homojunction
Photoelectric

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 51204129 and Open Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Raman spectrum excited by 633 nm lasers.
The AFM (a) image and TEM (b) image of GQDs.
The XRD patterns of GQDs/W18O49.
The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of W18O49 and GQDs doped W18O49.
The XRD patterns (a) and partial enlarged XRD patterns (b) of t-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
The FTIR spectra of GQDs, GQDs/W18O49, t-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
Survey (a) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (b), O 1s (c) and W 4f (d) for GQDs/W18O49; survey (e) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (f), O 1s (g) and W 4f (h) for GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
The TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) image of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 and the enlarged HRTEM image (c) of the dashed circle in (a).
The XRD pattern (a) and partial enlarged detail (b) of GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3; The XRD pattern (c) and partial enlarged detail (d) of GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3.
The EIS Nyquist plots of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3, GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3, GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
UV–Vis spectra (a) and the estimated band gaps (b) of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3, GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3.
The structure diagram of orthogonal WO3 (a), hexagonal WO3 (b) and tetragonal WO3 (c).
The EIS Nyquist plots (a) and Bode plots (b) of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-1:1, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1, and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-3:1.
The transient photocurrent responses curves of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-1:1, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1, and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-3:1.
Mott Schottky plots of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-1:1, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1, and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-3:1.
Schematic illustration of the charge-transfer in GQDs/W18O49/WO3 composites.
Degradation efficiency (a) and relationship curve (b) of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 in MB solution.
Schematic illustration of degradation of MB by GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 under visible light irradiation.
The list of references about tungsten oxide in introduction.
The lattice parameters and the band gap of o-WO3, h-WO3 and t-WO3.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of GQDs/W18O49/tetragonal WO3 homostructures for improving the photoelectric properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Non-ferrous Metals and Materials, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, Peoples R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as excellent conductive materials could effectively enhance the electron transmission efficiency of W18O49 nanofibers after their combination. In order to investigate the effect of different crystal phase WO3 on the optoelectric properties of GQDs/W18O49 composites, the orthogonal phase WO3 (o-WO3), hexagonal phase WO3 (h-WO3) and tetragonal phase WO3 (t-WO3) had been used to produce GQDs/W18O49/WO3 homojunction with GQDs/W18O49. After comparing them, it was found that the photocurrent intensity of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was 2.35 times, 1.68 times and 1.08 times as high as that of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 at 1.2 V versus Hg/Hg2Cl2, respectively. And the t-WO3 could effectively decrease the band gap, reduce the interfacial resistance, prolong the electron lifetime, accelerate the electron conduction and increase the photocurrent magnitude of the homojunction. The photoelectric performance of the composites could be adjusted by regulating the ratios of GQDs/W18O49 and t-WO3, the ratio 2:1 of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites presented a higher photocurrent density and lower interfacial resistance than other proportions. The physical mechanism of such enhanced photoconduction in this homostructure have been discussed.

ce:section.ce:para:
Recently, non-stoichiometric tungsten oxide (WO3−x) had gradually attracted the attention of researchers, such as WO2.9 (W20O58), WO2.8 (W5O14) and WO2.7 (W18O49). Among them, W18O49 as a potential semiconductor containing the most abundant oxygen defect, tungsten atom of multiple valences, and superior photoelectric characteristics [1–3]. Many researchers had done related research (
Table 1), among them, Mohamedkhair et al. fabricated a thin film of W18O49 could improve the electrical conductivity and the kinetics of charge separation [4]. Sinha et al. reported that oxygen vacancies and surface area could promote charge transportation of W18O49 by studying the mechanism of faradaic charge transfer [5]. As the light irradiated on W18O49 surface, the photo-generated electrons were restricted by a broad band-gap and fast recombination rate, which limited the electron-transfer of W18O49. To solve these obstacles, some attempts have been performed to incorporate W18O49 with other conductive materials. Feng et al. reported that the W18O49 wrapped by g-C3N4 could achieve lasting photo-catalysis driven by sunlight [6]. Sun et al. discovered oxygen vacancy-rich W18O49 had an excellent storage property when it applied to anode materials [7]. As a kind of new conductive materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) inherited the high specific surface area and good electron transmission performance of graphene, and made a great progress in the field of photocatalysis [8–11]. For example, Chang et al. reported that the introduction of GQDs can improve the photoactivity of TiO2 nanorods in hydrogen production by solar energy [12]. Tsai et al. presented that CdSe mediated by graphene quantum dots can improve electrochemical hydrogen production by enhancing electron transport [13]. In previous work, we employed a solvothermal method to obtained GQDs doped W18O49 composites [14]. The band-gap of the composites was narrowed, and the electron capture and transport abilities were improved due to the doping of GQDs.

Although the combination of W18O49 and GQDs seemed to improve charge separation by preventing charge recombination, there was still plenty of space in exploring W18O49 based hybrids with less electron-transfer resistance, such as modification of morphology [15,16], introduction of cocatalyst [17,18] and synthesis of heterojunction [19], and so on, in which establishment of heterojunction was an significant technique to improve performance, which could concentrate electrons and holes in different components, thus inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs and improving the electron transmission efficiency. Sen et al. researched and discovered that the SnO2/W18O49 heterojunction was a combination of high sensitivity and selectivity to the gas sensors [20]. Shen et al. reported that CeO2/W18O49 heterojunction could strengthen photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [21]. Hsu et al. systematically reviewed the development and mechanism of heterojunction in the field of photocatalytic application [22–25]. However, the heterojunction would increase the interfacial resistance of electron transmission between different substances, which hindered the improvement of electrochemical performance of heterojunction. In order to solve this problem, researchers put forward the concept of homojunction that composed of the same or similar semiconductors, which improved interface transmission properties of electrons under the action of built-in electric field [26–29]. Wang et al. found that W18O49/WO3 could be used as a high-performance gas sensor for NO2 Sensing [30]. Zhan et al. demonstrated that the WO3−x/WO3 homojunction possessed a great potential in the field of photo-anode [31]. Based on the reported work, there were some researches about GQDs doping or WO3−x based homojunction, but far less information was available integrating GQDs and W18O49/WO3 to improve photoelectric properties.

Herein, a continuing effort was proposed to combine GQDs/W18O49 with different crystalline phases (orthogonal phase, hexagonal phase and tetragonal phase) to reduce the energy band-gap, prevent the electron-hole recombination, and decrease charge-transfer resistance. GQDs/W18O49/WO3 composites were synthesized by a simple two-step hydrothermal method, and the composites were characterized by a series of electrochemical tests. The effects of different WO3 phases and proportions on the photoelectric properties of the homogeneous junction were discussed, and the electron transport mechanism was studied in detail.

Nitric acid (HNO3), natural flake graphite, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), tungsten hexachloride (WCl6), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), isopropanol ((CH3)2CHOH), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), methylene blue (MB) and propanone (C3H6O), urea (CON2H4). All the chemicals were analytical grade.

GO was prepared by the improved Hummers’ process. natural flake graphite (5 g), NaNO3 (2.5 g) and H2SO4 (115 ml) were mixed and stirred for 10 min at room temperature. Then, the mixture was kept at 4–6 °C for 1.5 h. Next, the solution was reacted at 38 °C for 0.5 h. Afterwards, deionized water (200 ml) and H2O2 (30 ml) were poured into the solution and kept at 95 °C for 0.5 h, cooled naturally, the production was washed and purified with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, the samples were dried to get GO.

GO (50 mg) was dispersed in HNO3 (50 ml) with ultrasonic reaction for 4 h, and the mixture was moved into a Teflon-lined 100 ml of autoclave and kept at 100 °C for 12 h. Next, the as-prepared samples were washed with deionized water and dried under ventilation conditions. Finally, the solid product was heated at 400 °C for 4 h in a quartz tubular furnace. The resulting products were GQDs.

Na2WO4·2H2O (0.5 g), CON2H4 (0.1 g) and C3H6O (15 ml) were dispersed in deionized water (18 ml) for stirring 10 min, and adjusted pH of the mixed solution to 1. Then the mix solution was moved into a Teflon-lined 100 ml of autoclave and kept at 140 °C for 72 h. The obtained products were centrifuged and washed with deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The obtained solid powder was orthogonal phase WO3 that named as o-WO3. Hexagonal phase WO3 and tetragonal phase WO3 were obtained by calcining o-WO3 at 450 °C and 600 °C, which named as h-WO3 and t-WO3, respectively.

Alcoholysis of WCl6 was used to prepare GQDs /W18O49. WCl6 powder (0.395 g) was dissolved in (CH3)2CHOH (40 ml), and then a certain weight of GQDs was dispersed in the mixture and stirred for 0.5 h. Next, the mixed solution was moved into a Teflon-lined 100 ml of autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 6 h. The products were purified by ethanol and deionized water, and dried at 60 °C for 8 h to get the GQDs/W18O49 composites (the mass ratio of GQDs to W18O49 was 1.3 wt%, 1.4 wt%, 1.5 wt%, 1.6 wt% and 1.7 wt%). For contrast, blank W18O49 samples were synthesized in the same process without GQDs.

The GQDs/W18O49/WO3 composites were synthesized by adding different crystalline phases of o-WO3, h-WO3 and t-WO3 during the synthesis process of GQDs/W18O49. The mass ratio of GQDs/W18O49 to t-WO3 (o-WO3 and h-WO3) was 1:1 and the composites were denoted as the performance of GQDs/W18O49/WO3 with different crystalline phases. GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites with different mass ratio were prepared by changing the ratios from 1:1, 2:1–3:1.

The chemical properties and morphology were characterized by XRD (RU-200B/D/MAX-RB), TEM (Tecnai G2 F20) and AFM (Multimode 8), respectively. The detailed chemical state of composites was characterized via an XPS (ESCALAB 250XI). FTIR spectra were explored on a Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer. The Raman spectra of composites were characterized on a RENISHAW Raman microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectra were performed on the UV5500 spectrophotometer. The photoelectric properties of samples were examined at electrochemical workstation (CHI650E). The photo-degradation research was carried out under light source of xenon lamp (MICROSOLAR300).

The photoelectrochemical measurements was measured by a CHI 650E workstation in a three-electrode system, the Pt and an Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode were served as the counter and reference electrode, respectively. The sample was coated on FTO glass with an effective area of 1 cm2 as a working electrode, and a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp was used as a point light source. The transient photocurrent responses, EIS Nyquist plots and linear sweep voltammetry curves of samples were measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. The LSV scan rate was 0.1 V/s, the potential applied for Nyquist plots was 0.4 V, and the potential for transient photocurrent responses for 0.4 V.

The light source was a 300 W Xe lamp, the light intensity was 60 cd, the current was 21.5 A, and the power was 300 W. The device was located at a distance of 20 cm from the liquid level of solution. Before the reaction, 15 mg of samples was added to 100 ml of 10 mg/L MB solution, and the mixture was stirred in a dark environment for 0.5 h to achieve adsorption equilibrium. Then, the mixture was kept at the speed of 500 r/min under the light source, 1 ml of the reaction solution was extracted every 10 min, and analyzed at the characteristic absorption peak of 664 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer.

As shown in Fig. 1, the D and G Raman peaks of GQDs were located at 1338 and 1594 cm−1 ascribed to the structural defects and the vibration in plane of sp2 C atoms [32], respectively. The intensity ratio of D and G peaks (ID/IG) as a symbol of the structural defect density of graphite materials was calculated to 0.97, which proved that the formation of low structural disorder and high steady structure in the high temperature annealing process.

In order to detect the particles size and morphological features of GQDs nanoparticles, the AFM image and TEM image are displayed in Fig. 2. Fig. 2a displays the AFM of GQDs with the height distribution, it can be directly recognized that the average thickness of GQDs was 1.5 nm corresponding to 2―3 layers of graphene sheets. The shape of GQDs is presented in Fig. 2b. It could be seen that the zero-dimension GQDs were evenly distributed with diameters within 4 nm.

To verify the composition of the GQDs/W18O49, the XRD pattern of the samples produced by the solvothermal method is shown in Fig. 3. It could be clearly discovered that the peaks located at 23.3°, 34.3° and 47.6° were ascribed to (010), (114) and (020) planes, which indexed to the monoclinic W18O49 (JCPDS NO. 71-2450), and a shoulder peak at around 28.3° was coincided with (203) plane of GQDs [33,34]. All these indicated the appearance of GQDs in GQDs/W18O49 composites.

As reported in previous literature [14], the modification of GQDs could significantly improve the electron-transfer performance of W18O49. In order to obtain the optimal electrochemistry properties, the effect of different GQDs doping amounts on the impedance of W18O49 was investigated and shown in Fig. 4. A smaller arc radius indicated a smaller impedance, and GQDs/W18O49 reached the smallest impedance at the doping content of 1.5 wt%. This result proved that GQDs had the excellent conductivity, which could quickly conduct charges and effectively reduce the interfacial charge-transfer resistance after the combination of W18O49. Moreover, the existence of GQDs enhanced the shielding effect and abated the exciton binding between electrons and hole pairs, thereby improving the ability of electron-hole pair separation and transmission of GQDs/W18O49 [35].

GQDs/W18O49 was combined with t-WO3 by a solvothermal approach, and the crystalline structures of t-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites were researched by XRD detection. As shown in
Fig. 5a, the four primary diffraction peaks of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 situated at 22.84°, 23.9°, 28.56° and 33.34° were indexed to the (001), (200), (111) and (201) of tetragonal WO3 (PDF NO. 89–1287). Moreover, the partial enlarged detail of XRD (Fig. 5b) showed that the characteristic peak at 23.3° ascribed to (010) plane of GQDs/W18O49 was presented in partial XRD pattern of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3, which proved that GQDs/W18O49 and t-WO3 were successfully compounded. Furthermore, according to the formula: crystallinity = (area of all crystalline peaks/ area of all peaks) × 100%, in which crystalline peaks were long-range and ordered peaks, and all peaks included crystalline peaks and amorphous peaks (short-range and disorder) [36,37]. The calculation result of annealed composites was 88.42%, suggesting that as-prepared GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 had a high crystallinity.

The functional groups of GQDs, GQDs/W18O49, t-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites were characterized by FTIR analysis. In the spectra of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3, the vibration peaks of 3420 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 were originated from the stretching and bending modes of the -OH [38,39]. The peak at 1390 cm−1 was due to the stretch vibration of C-H. The absorption bond from 500 cm−1 to 1000 cm−1 was associated with various W-O bonds, which came from the W18O49 and WO3 together. Besides, the peaks at 1730 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the CO and C-O bonds, which was absent in the spectrum of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites since the amount of GQDs was too little to be detected [40].

In order to analyze the chemical state in surface and chemical composition of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites, the survey and high-resolution XPS spectra are performed. As shown in Figs. 7a and e, the survey XPS spectrum detected the binding energies consistent with O 1s, W 4p, C1s, W 4d and W4f, which were originated from GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 7b) were split into two main peaks at the binding energy of 284.6 eV and 286.16 eV corresponding to C-C and CO [41], respectively. It was worth noting that the highest peak (284.6 eV) of GQDs/W18O49 (Fig. 7b) and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 (Fig. 7f) composites was located at the same binding energy, which indicated that the correction of all XPS spectrograms were carried out under the same standard. Fig. 7c shows the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s with peaks at binding energy of 533.38 eV, 531.94 eV and 530.91 eV, which was attributed to lattice oxygen ions, W-O-W bonds and oxygen defect [42]. In Fig. 7d, the W 4f spectra were fitted into four peaks, where the stronger peaks centered at 38.25 eV and 36.10 eV can be attributed to the W6+, and the weaker peaks at 37.47 eV and 34.60 eV belonged to the W5+ [43–45]. After the formation of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 homojunction, the binding energy (Fig. 7g) of O 1 s was shifted to 532.94 eV and 531.34 eV and 530.62 eV, and the binding energy (Fig. 7h) of W6+ was shifted to 38.18 eV and 36.04 eV, and that of W5+ was shifted to 37.44 eV and 34.57 eV. By comparing the high-resolution O 1s and W 4f spectra of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3, the binding energy presented a slight shift to lower values, probably owing to interfacial charge transfer from t-WO3 to GQDs/W18O49. The transmission of electrons between different components caused the electron density of GQDs/W18O49/ t-WO3 to increase, the O 1s values and W 4f values were reduced under the influence of the negative correlation between binding energy and electron density. Moreover, the charge transfer indicated the presence of built-in electric field between the GQDs/W18O49 and the WO3, which proved that the formation of homojunction [46].

The morphology and structure of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 were explored by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 8a, the pure WO3 was nanoparticle shape and W18O49 was nanofiber shape with a length of 300 nm. The HRTEM images of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 are shown in Figs. 8b and c. The crystal distance of 0.24 nm, 0.38 nm and 0.37 nm were separately indexed to the (1120), (010) and (200) lattice planes of GQDs, W18O49 and WO3, indicating a good combination between GQDs/W18O49 and t-WO3. GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 (Fig. 8b) still showed the clear lattice stripes at a high magnification of 5 nm, indicating that GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 had a better lattice quality [46]. In addition, the tight interfaces (Fig. 8a) and well lattice matching (Fig. 8c) could be clearly seen, so that the van der Waals homostructures was successfully constructed by W18O49 and WO3, which was beneficial to the efficient electron transport of tungsten oxide homojunction [47,48].

To compare the chemical properties of GQDs/W18O49/different phase WO3, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 were synthesized by the same solvothermal process. The XRD patterns of orthogonal and hexagonal phase WO3 combined with GQDs/W18O49 are shown in Figs. 9a and c, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9a, the characteristic peaks at 13.84°, 16.36°, 23.22° and 27.94° were indexed to (020), (111), (002), (222) planes of orthogonal phase WO3 (PDF NO. 87-1203). After annealing at 450 °C, the characteristic peaks of the composites changed obviously, and the peaks at 13.8°, 22.98° and 25.44° were consistent with (100), (001) and (110) planes of hexagonal phase WO3 (PDF NO. 75-2187). It can be observed that the characteristic peak within 20° gradually disappeared, and the peaks (Fig. 9b) within 20–30° were evolved into a stronger peak corresponding to (001) plane and weaker peaks corresponding to (110), (101) and (200) planes. When the annealing temperature was raised to 600 °C, the XRD pattern of the composites was changed into the tetragonal phase. As shown in Fig. 5a, the peaks within 20° disappeared completely, and a strong peak corresponding to (200) plane appeared along the growth direction. Additionally, the characteristic peak at 23.3° corresponding to (010) plane of W18O49 appeared in partial enlarged detail of XRD as shown in Figs. 9b and d, which proved that the successful combination of GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 composites.

The effect of different crystal phases WO3 on GQDs/W18O49 was investigated by a series of electrochemical tests. The EIS spectra of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3, GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 are shown in Fig. 10. The EIS Nyquist plots were superior to those of GQDs/W18O49, and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 provided the minimum charge-transfer impedance. In the LSV curves ( Fig. 11), the photo-current intensity of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was 2.35 times, 1.68 times and 1.08 times as high as that of GQDs/W18O49, GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3 and GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3 at 1.2 V versus Hg/Hg2Cl2, respectively. This result was consistent with that of EIS. It indicated that the formation of homojunction was conducive to reduce the electron transfer resistances, promote the accumulation of electrons and holes in VB and CB [49–51], accelerate the electron transfer rate, and thus improve the electrochemical properties of the composites. The intrinsic properties of the composites were analyzed by UV–Vis spectra ( Fig. 12a). Compared with pure GQDs/W18O49, the absorption tail of GQDs/W18O49/WO3 homojunction displayed the red shift at different degree. The estimated corresponding band gap of the composites was researched in Fig. 12b. On the basis of GQDs/W18O49, the band gap was narrowed with the introduction of orthogonal phase WO3, hexagonal phase WO3 and tetragonal phase WO3. In the calculation of Migas and Jin Hua (Table 1), the band gap of o-WO3, h-WO3 and t-WO3 were obtained by using different calculation methods. Among them, the band gap of o-WO3 was the widest, t-WO3 was the narrowest, and the band gap of metastable phase h-WO3 was between that of o-WO3 and t-WO3 and closed to that of t-WO3 [52,53]. This result was consistent with the law obtained by UV-Vis (Fig. 12). The combination of WO3 with different phases and GQDs/W18O49 affected the overall the conduction band minimum (VBM) to varying degrees hence affecting the band gap [35]. The band gap sequence from small to large was: GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3< GQDs/W18O49/h-WO3< GQDs/W18O49/o-WO3. This phenomenon might be ascribed two reasons. On the one hand, the structure evolution was associated with the crystalline phase and space group. At the annealing temperature of 450 °C, the crystalline phase and space group of o-WO3 ( Fig. 13a) was changed from orthogonal phase and Fmm2 to hexagonal phase (Fig. 13b) and P6/mmm. While the crystalline phase and space group were transferred to the tetragonal phase (Fig. 13c) and P-421m under the annealing condition of 600 °C. This structure evolution gradually increased the symmetry of crystal, shortened the distance between W and O atoms, realigned energy-heightening of the WO6 units [54], and led to the reduction of band gap and electron transmission path of t-WO3. After t-WO3 was compounded with GQDs/W18O49, the junction structure formed by band bending can further promote the electron transmission efficiency and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, a unified Fermi level would be formed when GQDs/W18O49 and WO3 of different crystal phases were combined to form the homojunction, WO3 with different band gaps would affect the Fermi level position of the whole compound. When t-WO3 with the narrowest band gap was compounded with GQDs/W18O49, it would greatly weaken the blocking effect of potential energy barrier between difference materials, narrow the band gap position and improve the photoelectric performance of the composites [55,56].

GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites with the mass ratio of 1:1, 2:1 and 3:1 were tested by electrochemical impedance analysis. As shown in Fig. 14a, the R1 and CPE1 represented the physical resistance of FTO glasses and material films and electrolyte solution, and double layer capacitance, correspondingly. In addition, the W1 was the symbol of diffusion resistance that correlated with the diffusion of —OH. The interfacial resistance R2 between composites and solution could be tested by comparing the size of arc radius at high-frequency district in the Nyquist plots [57–59]. As the mass ratio was changed from 1:1–3:1, the arcs radius of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was decreased at first and then increased. The electron-transfer resistance of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 reached the minimum at the ratio of 2:1. To further research the separation and transmission rate of the charges, the average lifetime of charges was revealed through the Bode plots (Fig. 14b). According to the formula: τ = 1/ (2πƒ p), τ is the average lifetime, ƒ p is the frequency of the highest peak [30]. The calculation from smaller frequency to larger frequency in order was as follows: 0.42 s, 0.13 s, 0.11 s and 0.03 s. The results showed that the composites at the ratio of 2:1 could effectively prolong the average lifetime of charges, promote the accumulation of electrons and holes in the CB and VB respectively, and enhance the separation and transmission rate of charges. Besides, the GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites with a suitable ratio had a better electrochemical performance. The trap caused by sub-stoichiometric defects in W18O49 can be used as the trapping center of electrons, which promoted the transmission of electrons and the separation of carriers. And the oxygen vacancy in W18O49 can produce the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect in the near infrared region, and the ability of capturing photons and the efficiency of carrier separation could be improved under the synergistic effect of the defects and LSPR [60]. The proper defect concentration can effectively improve photoelectric properties of homojunction.

The transient photocurrent responses curves of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 compounds with different mass ratios are exhibited in Fig. 15. All of samples have sensitive instantaneous photocurrent responses at 365 nm ultraviolet light radiation. Obviously, the photocurrent density of the GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites was stronger than that of GQDs/W18O49, indicating that the formation of tungsten oxide homojunction could improve the photoelectric properties. Besides, the mass ratios had an impact on the photocurrent density of compounds. The photocurrent density of the composites first increased as the ratio increased, achieved the optimal value at the ratio of 2:1, and then decreased when the synthetic proportion was further increased from 2:1–3:1. The photocurrent density of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1 achieved 1.4 μA/cm2, which was 3.59 times as high as that of the GQDs/W18O49.The enhancement was ascribed to the addition of proper WO3 which could make the energy band bend, and adjust oxygen vacancy concentration of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites to improve the separation and transmission of photo-generated electron pairs.

Fig. 16 presented the Mott Schottky diagram of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites at different mass ratios soaked in 0.1 M Na2SO4 at a fixed frequency of 2 kHz. The slope of the linear part of the Mott Schottky curves was shown in the inset of Fig. 16. The slope was inversely proportional to the carrier density, which meant that the carrier density of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1 was superior to that of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 at ratios of 1:1 and 3:1. This rule was consistent with the results of EIS and transient photocurrent responses. There were two reasons for maximizing the photoelectric activity of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3-2:1. Firstly, the oxygen vacancy concentration of the composites was changed by the form of mass ratio, which regulated the energy level of defects and improved the energy utilization efficiency during electronic transition. Moreover, oxygen vacancy from W18O49 can produce the localized surface plasmon resonance to improve the ability of capturing photons and separating carriers. Secondly, when the mass ratio of W18O49 to t-WO3 was appropriate, junction structure induced the construction of built-in electric field, and the energy bands between different components can be tuned to the optimum, thus promoting the carrier transmission efficiency.

To explain the charge-transfer of GQDs/W18O49/WO3 composites, the mechanism was investigated. According to references [48], the VB and CB position of W18O49 was 3.24 eV and 0.42 eV, the VB and CB position of WO3 was 2.92 eV and −0.03 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 17, W18O49 was modified by GQDs that played the role of free electron capture center, which promoted electron transport of GQDs/W18O49. After forming the homojunction with WO3, a stronger built-in electric field was formed when the energy band was bent or interfacial electric field was gradient, which promoted the interacial accumulation and transfer of electrons and holes, prolonged the lifetime of electrons, led to the formation of new Fermi energy level, and enhanced the electron transport properties of GQDs/W18O49/WO3 composites.

In order to explore the practical application of samples, 15 mg of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 were added in 10 mg/L MB to analyze the degradation rate. In Fig. 18a, the degradation rates of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 were 49.76% and 24.94% in dark reaction for 30 min, correspondingly. After irradiation with xenon lamp for 50 min, the degradation rate of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was 86.93% and 12.01% more than GQDs/W18O49, indicating that the compounds could increase the degradation rate. In the relationship curve (Fig. 18b), the reaction rate constant of GQDs/W18O49 and GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 was 0.02251 and 0.02677, indicating the tungsten oxide homojunction could strengthen the photo-degradation.

The mechanism of photo-degradation of MB was illustrated in Fig. 19. Under the irradiation of visible light, the electrons (e-) were induced to come out from low-priced W5+, and then this W5+ was converted into high-priced W6+. Then, the photo-induced electrons in the VB of W18O49 were transferred to GQDs, which effectively enhanced the separation of electron-hole pairs. Immediately, superoxide radicals (∙O2 -) were generated by reducing O2 dissolved in the solution and driven by electrons in CB. In addition, the active hydroxyl (∙OH) was produced by H2O together with excited holes (h+) that played as an oxidant in the VB of WO3, which directly reacted with MB molecules.

In summary, GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites were synthesized by a solvothermal process with the GQDs amount of 1.5 wt%. Based on the characterization results, the GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites grew along the (200) plane, and consisted of nanoparticle shape WO3 and nanofiber shape W18O49. The similar lattice direction and crystal plane spacing of GQDs/W18O49 and t-WO3 were conducive to electrons transfer of homojunction. GQDs/W18O49/different phase WO3 composites were compared by EIS, LSV and UV-Vis analysis. The results showed that the electrochemical properties of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 were superior to those of others due to the reduced band gap or interfacial modulation in nanoscale, the prolonged electron lifetime and the promoted electron transfer. The GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 with ratio 2:1 processed the smallest electron-transfer resistance, the longest average lifetime and the highest photocurrent density of 1.4 μA/cm2 which was 3.59 times as high as that of the GQDs/W18O49. The existence of built-in electric field and the bending of energy band accelerated the migration of electrons and shorten the migration distance, which improved the photoelectric properties of GQDs/W18O49/t-WO3 composites. This work provided a useful insight for the development of tungsten oxide in the practical electrochemical fields as well as the exploration about the homojunction.

Yun Lei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Peng Du: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yuncui Wu: Investigation. Can Li, Beibei Du, Yongqin Wang, Linhui Luo: Visualization. Bingsuo Zou: Revision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 51204129 and Open Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09).
